title: From Network Models to Intelligent Cyber Control
takeaway: Use the three repositories as one learning path: model, solve, learn, validate, and write.
body: Foundation, game learning, and PINN/PIDL companions
Deakin-style student onboarding deck
Built from the current public repositories and generated figures
notes: Open with the idea that the repos are not separate demos. They are a staged route from equations to paper-level experiments.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Learning outcomes
takeaway: After this deck, students should know where to start and what to change next.
body: Run the first smoke tests from a clean checkout.
Map each equation term to a Python variable and output figure.
Choose between PMP/FBSM, RL/MARL, PINN/PIDL, and hybrid games.
Design baselines, ablations, and paper-ready figures.
notes: Emphasize practical fluency: run, inspect, modify, validate.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: The project pipeline
takeaway: Every example follows the same research pattern.
body: State the cyber process and control surface.
Pick a solver or learner that matches timing and information.
Compare with baselines and write the evidence trail.
notes: Use this as the mental map for all later sections.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Three repositories at a glance
takeaway: The family is staged: foundation first, then game learning, then PINN/PIDL.
body: Foundation: notation, shared package, continuous/impulse/hybrid examples.
Note 1: ODE-to-MDP, DDQN, compact CTDE, node-SIPRS MAPPO.
Note 2: inverse PINN, PIDL, neural control, PMP-informed residuals.
notes: Make clear that Note 1 and Note 2 build on the foundation package instead of redefining the basic cyber dynamics.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Recommended learning sequence
takeaway: Students should run before extending.
body: First 15 minutes: install and run smoke examples.
First week: trace one equation to code and change one parameter.
Second week: add a baseline and explain the output figure.
Project stage: extend states, agents, losses, or graph settings.
notes: This slide sets expectations for students who may otherwise jump straight to a complex paper model.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Prerequisites and environment
takeaway: The examples are intentionally small, but the workflow is research-shaped.
body: Python 3.10+ with NumPy, SciPy, Matplotlib, pandas, and PyTorch for neural examples.
Optional python-igraph for scale-free graph generation and scalability figures.
Device setting: auto picks MPS, CUDA, then CPU when supported.
Use a sibling-folder workspace for all three repos.
notes: Point students to the README quick starts. Hardware acceleration helps for neural diagnostics, but smoke tests are CPU-friendly.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Workspace and install
takeaway: Keep the foundation package installed once and reused by both companion notes.
body: git clone https://github.com/LYang910920/network-control-differential-games
git clone https://github.com/LYang910920/note1-cyber-control-games
git clone https://github.com/LYang910920/note2-pinn-pidl-cyber-control
cd note1-cyber-control-games
pip install -e ../network-control-differential-games
pip install -r requirements.txt
notes: Explain that editable install makes shared fixes immediately visible to Note 1 and Note 2.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: The first 15-minute success path
takeaway: A fast smoke run gives students a known-good baseline.
body: cd network-control-differential-games
pip install -e .
python run_all.py --skip-reference --skip-scalability
cd ../note1-cyber-control-games
bash scripts/run_smoke_tests.sh
cd ../note2-pinn-pidl-cyber-control
bash scripts/run_smoke_tests.sh
notes: The point is not full convergence; it is to confirm the environment and locate the first generated figures.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Read equations and code together
takeaway: Every mathematical object should have a concrete code location.
body: Equation: state, control, parameter, payoff, constraint.
Code: RHS function, solver step, update rule, metric, plot label.
Output: CSV row, figure caption, and README interpretation.
notes: This sets up the notation-to-code habit students need before modifying models.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Shared notation and state shapes
takeaway: Most mistakes are shape mistakes, so name the shape first.
body: Aggregate: one population vector.
Degree level: one state per degree class k.
Node level: one state vector per node i.
Graph pressure: A[i,j] contributes from j to i.
notes: Reinforce that labels and captions must say selected node, selected degree, or aggregate mean.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Model granularity
takeaway: Aggregate, degree-level, and node-level models answer different questions.
body: Aggregate: compact and transparent.
Degree level: keeps heterogeneity by k while staying low-dimensional.
Node level: keeps topology and local interventions at higher cost.
notes: Connect this to the earlier scalability work: node-level FBS is expensive because the state dimension grows with nodes.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: SIR, SIPS, and SIPRS
takeaway: The state diagram tells students what each compartment can and cannot do.
body: SIR: susceptible, infected, recovered.
SIPS: susceptible, infected, patched, susceptible again.
SIPRS: adds recovered or aware state and waning transitions.
notes: Explain that P is a patch/protected state, not the same as R recovery/awareness.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Network pressure convention
takeaway: The code uses one explicit adjacency direction.
body: A[i,j] is the influence from node j into node i.
Pressure at node i is a weighted sum over neighbor infection states.
Normalize pressure when comparing graph sizes.
Sparse and dense paths should match on small tests.
notes: This prevents transposed adjacency bugs when students move to paper-level graph data.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Continuous control
takeaway: A continuous control is a time-indexed curve, not a single scalar.
body: The solver samples u(t) on a mesh.
The true interpretation is a continuously adjustable intervention.
Plots should show the curve against time, with units or normalized intensity.
notes: Contrast this with the sampled learning action later; sampled decisions are held or applied at epochs.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Sampled decisions versus solver substeps
takeaway: RL decisions happen at t_k; RK4 substeps only integrate the flow.
body: Observe x(t_k).
Choose action a_k.
Integrate ODE with internal substeps.
Return x(t_{k+1}).
notes: This distinction matters for DDQN, CTDE, and MAPPO examples.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Impulse and hybrid control
takeaway: Impulse control changes the state at event times; hybrid control combines jumps and flow.
body: Continuous: u(t) changes rates over time.
Impulse: x(tau_j+) = G(x(tau_j-), q_j).
Hybrid: flow between impulses plus jumps at selected times.
notes: Mention that captions and labels should explicitly say continuous, impulse, or hybrid.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Single-controller objective
takeaway: Optimal-control examples balance cyber harm against intervention cost.
body: State exposure: infected, compromised, or propaganda-active mass.
Control cost: patching, filtering, isolation, or awareness effort.
Terminal cost: final risk or cleanup burden.
Constraints: state simplex and bounded control.
notes: Use this to introduce why baselines matter: reducing infection by spending infinite control is not a useful method.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Differential-game payoff
takeaway: A game adds strategic response to the same dynamical system.
body: Defender action reduces harm but costs resources.
Attacker action increases pressure but also costs effort.
Open-loop candidates need unilateral-deviation checks before strong claims.
Response matrices are clearer than isolated payoff numbers.
notes: Keep the claim discipline explicit. Avoid saying Nash unless the evidence supports it.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Open-loop and feedback strategies
takeaway: Open-loop plans depend on time; feedback policies also observe the state.
body: Open-loop: u(t) is computed before deployment.
Feedback: pi(x,t) reacts to observed state.
FBSM often returns open-loop schedules.
RL/MARL examples learn sampled feedback policies.
notes: This prepares the comparison between nominal-beta FBSM and feedback DDQN robustness.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Method-selection map
takeaway: Choose the method from data, control timing, and information structure.
body: Known equations + continuous control -> PMP/FBSM.
Sampled decision-making -> DDQN or CTDE/MAPPO.
Sparse data or missing mechanism -> PINN/PIDL.
Jump interventions -> impulse or hybrid games.
notes: Students should be able to justify method choice before coding.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Foundation repo code map
takeaway: The foundation repo owns the canonical shared pieces.
body: src/cybercontrol: reusable numerics, dynamics, diagnostics, Torch helpers.
examples/foundations: beginner-readable continuous, game, node, and hybrid examples.
examples/reference: small smoke runs for paper-level code snapshots.
notes: Point out that Note 1 and Note 2 import these utilities to keep semantics aligned.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: PMP intuition
takeaway: Costates measure how future cost changes when the state is perturbed.
body: Forward: simulate the state under a candidate control.
Backward: propagate marginal value information.
Update: improve control from the Hamiltonian condition.
Repeat until the control update is small.
notes: Do not over-formalize. This is intuition before equations.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: PMP optimality system
takeaway: The numerical solver follows the state, costate, and stationarity equations.
body: state:      x_dot = f(x, u, theta)
costate:   lambda_dot = -H_x(x, u, lambda)
control:   H_u(x, u, lambda) = 0
bounds:    u_min <= u(t) <= u_max
diagnose:  ||u_new - u_old|| over iterations
notes: Tie each line to code: RHS, adjoint, projection, and convergence plot.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: FBSM loop
takeaway: FBSM alternates forward simulation, backward adjoints, and relaxed updates.
body: Initialize control.
Forward integrate state.
Backward integrate costate.
Update and project control.
Check convergence.
notes: The iteration axis in convergence figures is the algorithm loop, not physical time.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: FBSM code-to-math walkthrough
takeaway: The same model terms appear in RHS, adjoint, update, metric, and plot label.
body: for it in range(max_iter):
x = forward_state(u)
lam = backward_costate(x, u)
u_candidate = hamiltonian_update(x, lam)
u = relax_and_project(u, u_candidate)
history.append(norm(u - u_old))
notes: This is pseudocode, not a verbatim source listing. It shows where students should inspect the real functions.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Degree versus node-level scalability
takeaway: The same epidemic-control problem becomes much larger at node level.
body: Same synthetic scale-free graph seed for each size.
Degree-level state uses observed degree classes.
Node-level state uses one entry per graph node.
notes: Use the actual checked-in scalability figure. Stress that log scaling helps compare 100 to 1,000,000 nodes.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Hybrid/impulse workflow
takeaway: Hybrid examples must label both continuous flow and discrete jumps.
body: Continuous dynamics evolve between event times.
Impulse events are shown as vertical markers.
Captions say whether curves are node, degree-class, or aggregate averages.
notes: This reinforces the user's prior requirement about clear labels and captions.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Foundation hands-on checkpoint
takeaway: Students should leave the foundation repo able to change one model responsibly.
body: Run python run_all.py --skip-reference --skip-scalability.
Open docs/NOTATION_TO_CODE.md and trace one term.
Change beta or cost weight and predict the figure change.
Add one baseline and update the caption.
notes: This checkpoint is intentionally concrete.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Why convert the ODE to an MDP?
takeaway: Learning methods need observations, actions, rewards, and transitions.
body: Continuous model gives the flow.
Environment wrapper selects decision epochs.
Reward summarizes cyber harm and action cost.
Policy learns from sampled rollouts.
notes: This is the conceptual bridge from optimal control to RL.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: One sampled transition
takeaway: A learning step wraps a continuous-time simulation interval.
body: observe x(t_k-)
choose action
apply jump if any
flow to t_{k+1}
return reward and next state
notes: Use t_k for decision epochs and tau_j for exogenous or designed impulse points.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: State, observation, action, reward
takeaway: Good RL code states exactly what the agent can see and change.
body: State: simulator variables used by the environment.
Observation: features exposed to the policy.
Action: discrete or continuous intervention choice.
Reward: signed scalar aligned with cyber objective.
notes: This is where terms like nominal and unknown proxy must be clear in docs.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: DDQN architecture
takeaway: DDQN is the beginner-friendly bridge from policy rules to learned feedback.
body: Replay buffer stabilizes updates.
Target network slows the moving target.
Evaluation uses deterministic rollouts separate from training noise.
notes: Use the existing Note 1 architecture figure.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: DDQN run command
takeaway: The smoke path is short; the longer diagnostics are optional.
body: cd note1-cyber-control-games
bash scripts/run_smoke_tests.sh
python scripts/generate_figures.py
python scripts/run_training_iterations.py --profile quick
python scripts/run_training_iterations.py --profile gpu --device auto
notes: Explain that smoke tests confirm code paths; longer profiles show learning behavior.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: From one defender to multiple agents
takeaway: Multi-agent control starts by deciding what is local and what is shared.
body: Agent: community, region, node group, or player.
Local observation: what each agent sees.
Centralized critic input: global training context.
Evaluation: decentralized policies acting from local observations.
notes: This slide introduces CTDE without calling every multi-agent baseline MAPPO.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Markov game and CTDE
takeaway: A Markov game adds multiple policies and coupled rewards.
body: Each agent chooses an action at the same decision epoch.
The environment applies joint action and continuous flow.
Training may use centralized information.
Deployment should respect the observation design.
notes: Stress the distinction between concept and algorithm.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Compact CTDE versus full MAPPO
takeaway: Do not call a compact baseline MAPPO unless it has the MAPPO machinery.
body: Compact CTDE: small actor/critic demonstration, short logs, limited claims.
Full MAPPO: rollout buffer, GAE, clipping, entropy/value losses, minibatch epochs, masks.
notes: This responds directly to the prior audit requirement.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: MAPPO rollout and update
takeaway: MAPPO separates data collection from clipped policy updates.
body: Collect rollout.
Compute returns and GAE.
Shuffle minibatches.
Update actor and critic with clipping.
Evaluate on fixed seeds.
notes: Define rollout as a complete sequence under a policy, not just one transition.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Node-SIPRS community environment
takeaway: The node-level SIPRS wrapper is the bridge to paper-level graph MARL.
body: Nodes carry S, I, P, and R shares or probabilities.
Communities map node groups to defender agents.
Actions allocate patching, filtering, or recovery effort.
Rewards combine infected exposure and intervention costs.
notes: Keep this aligned with the foundation network_models.py specification.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Cooperative community defense
takeaway: The cooperative setting asks regions to reduce global exposure under local observations.
body: The smoke baseline is small enough to inspect.
Use multiple seeds before making learning claims.
Report infection exposure, cost, peak, final state, and constraint errors.
notes: Use the training diagnostics as a visual anchor while warning about stochastic learning curves.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Attacker-defender response example
takeaway: Game outputs should compare responses, not only one learned curve.
body: Fix attacker and vary defender policies.
Fix defender and vary attacker policies.
Use payoff or exposure metrics with clear directionality.
notes: This matches the user's request for baseline comparison framing.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Evaluation terms
takeaway: Use plain definitions before showing training curves.
body: Nominal: parameter setting used for training or planning.
Robustness: lower exposure under specified mismatch, not a guarantee.
Unknown proxy: observable feature used when the true hidden count is unavailable.
Rollout: simulator trajectory under a fixed policy or parameter set.
notes: These terms were previously flagged as unclear; the deck repeats the clarified definitions.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Game-learning hands-on checkpoint
takeaway: Students should be able to add one policy and one metric.
body: Run bash scripts/run_smoke_tests.sh.
Open docs/PARAMETERS.md before changing hyperparameters.
Add a rule-based baseline in the policy comparison table.
Explain whether the curve is training, evaluation, or rollout behavior.
notes: Give a concrete end state for Note 1.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Why physics-informed learning?
takeaway: PINN/PIDL methods use equations when data are sparse, noisy, or partial.
body: Data loss fits observations.
Residual loss penalizes violation of known dynamics.
Boundary or initial losses anchor the trajectory.
Control or PMP losses encode decision structure.
notes: This orients students before the neural examples.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: PINN as constrained regression
takeaway: The network is trained to fit data and satisfy differential constraints.
body: Neural state function.
Autograd derivative.
ODE residual.
Data and boundary loss.
Validation rollout.
notes: Emphasize that residual consistency is not the same as real-world validation.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Forward, inverse, control, PMP-informed tasks
takeaway: The same building blocks support several learning questions.
body: Forward PINN: learn state trajectory with known parameters.
Inverse PINN: estimate hidden parameters from sparse data.
Control PINN: learn control and state networks together.
PMP-informed PINN: add costate and stationarity residuals.
notes: Students should name the task before choosing a loss.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Inverse PINN sparse-observation example
takeaway: Sparse observations become usable when the residual supplies structure.
body: Observed points are not a full trajectory.
The residual links hidden states and parameters.
Held-out state error checks whether the reconstruction is meaningful.
notes: Use the actual Note 2 figure.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: PIDL: known mechanism plus correction
takeaway: PIDL is useful when part of the mechanism is trusted and part is missing.
body: Keep known SIR/SIPRS terms explicit.
Train a correction network for the missing term.
Evaluate learned correction and rollout behavior separately.
notes: Make the known-plus-unknown split concrete.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Direct neural control
takeaway: Direct control PINNs optimize a neural policy against dynamics and objective terms.
body: State network approximates trajectory.
Control network outputs bounded intervention intensity.
Objective loss represents exposure plus effort.
Constraint penalties keep state and control feasible.
notes: Direct control is not automatically PMP-consistent; that comes in the next slide.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: PMP-informed residuals
takeaway: PMP-informed PINNs add optimality conditions to the training objective.
body: loss = data_loss
loss += w_ode * ||x_t - f(x,u)||^2
loss += w_adj * ||lambda_t + H_x||^2
loss += w_stat * ||H_u||^2
loss += w_bc * boundary_loss
notes: These are schematic terms. Students should inspect the actual loss names in docs/PARAMETERS.md.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Node/graph SIPRS extension
takeaway: The graph inverse PINN bridge reuses the same node-SIPRS equations.
body: Generate synthetic truth from the shared graph simulator.
Observe sparse nodes, states, or times.
Train against data, graph residual, and constraint losses.
Report state, parameter, residual, and held-out graph metrics.
notes: This is the route from toy inverse PINN to paper-level graph experiments.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Evaluation with ground truth
takeaway: Synthetic truth lets students measure errors directly.
body: Use held-out time points and nodes.
Track separate losses rather than one aggregate loss.
Compare to interpolation, wrong-parameter rollout, and known-only baselines.
notes: This slide encourages honest metrics.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Evaluation without ground truth
takeaway: When real truth is unavailable, use residual checks and predictive validation.
body: Held-out observations: does the model predict unseen data?
Residual consistency: does it obey known equations?
Rollout stability: does the learned mechanism produce plausible trajectories?
notes: Avoid claiming identifiability without stronger evidence.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: PINN/PIDL hands-on checkpoint
takeaway: Students should be able to add one loss term and one validation metric.
body: Run bash scripts/run_smoke_tests.sh.
Open docs/PARAMETERS.md for loss weights and network widths.
Remove or change one loss term and predict the failure mode.
Add a held-out metric before increasing model complexity.
notes: Give students a safe extension route.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: One cross-repo research question
takeaway: Paper-level work needs model, method, baselines, and evidence.
body: Foundation: define the node-SIPRS model.
Note 1: learn feedback/game policies.
Note 2: infer unknown graph mechanisms.
Paper: compare under clear limits.
notes: This is the cross-repo synthesis slide.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: From model to paper
takeaway: The final deliverable is a disciplined evidence chain, not just a trained model.
body: Question
Equations
Baselines
Method
Ablations
Validation
Figures
Claims
notes: Close the core deck by sending students to docs/from_model_to_paper.md and companion PAPER_WORKFLOW.md files.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Command cheat sheet
takeaway: Most workflows use a small set of repeatable commands.
body: python run_all.py --skip-reference --skip-scalability
bash scripts/run_smoke_tests.sh
python scripts/generate_figures.py
python scripts/run_training_iterations.py --profile quick
pytest
python -m compileall .
notes: Use this as the live-demo slide if time is short.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: File and function index
takeaway: The fastest way to extend is to start from the right file.
body: Foundation: src/cybercontrol/network_models.py.
Foundation examples: examples/foundations/code/.
Note 1 profiles: src/scenario_profiles.py.
Note 2 profiles: src/experiment_profiles.py.
notes: Students should not start by editing generated outputs.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Hyperparameter starting points
takeaway: Keep hyperparameters visible and small before scaling.
body: Read docs/PARAMETERS.md first in each repo.
Smoke runs use short horizons and few iterations by design.
Neural diagnostics expose learning rate, width, epochs, seeds, and device.
Scale one axis at a time: graph size, horizon, seeds, or network width.
notes: This aligns with the user's request that parameters and hyperparameters be easy to see.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Common errors and debugging
takeaway: Most failures are environment, shape, or timing mismatches.
body: Import error: install the foundation package in editable mode.
Shape error: check aggregate, degree, or node-level state dimensions.
Flat control curve: check cost weights and bounds.
Unclear plot: fix label before changing the method.
notes: Frame debugging as a reproducibility habit.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Glossary
takeaway: Use terms consistently across notes and figures.
body: Nominal: parameter setting used for planning or training.
Rollout: simulator trajectory under fixed policy/parameters.
Robustness: performance under a named shift, not a proof.
Hybrid: continuous flow plus discrete jumps.
notes: This reinforces the terminology cleanup in the repositories.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.

title: Repository URLs and references
takeaway: The public repos are the authoritative source for commands and figures.
body: Foundation URL: see the README.
Note 1 and Note 2 URLs: see related repos.
Reference snapshots keep upstream notices.
Do not redistribute private templates without rights.
notes: End with the licensing reminder: do not redistribute private templates or third-party assets without rights.
citation: Repository files and generated figures from the three-repo tutorial family.
